Common Causes

Why Garage Doors Go Off-Track

A door off its tracks is always an emergency — never force it open or closed. Call us for same-day repair before the damage gets worse.

Broken Cable or Spring

The most common cause. When a cable or spring snaps, the door loses balanced support and one side slips off the track immediately.

Vehicle Impact

A car hitting the door or track is a leading cause of off-track doors. Even a minor bump can bend the track enough to derail the rollers.

Worn or Broken Rollers

Old, cracked rollers can slip out of the track during operation. Once one roller fails, the door panel shifts and derails completely.

Bent or Misaligned Track

Tracks can bend from impact or years of stress. Even a slight bend creates a point where rollers catch, jam, and eventually jump the track.

Obstruction in the Track

Debris, dirt buildup, or a small object lodged in the track can force the door off its path mid-operation — causing sudden derailment.

Forcing a Stuck Door

Forcing a door that's stuck or jammed is one of the most preventable causes. It bends panels and tracks and almost always makes the repair much more expensive.

Our Process

How We Fix a Garage Door Off-Track

Off-track repair requires patience and precision — our technicians follow a safe, systematic process to get your door back on track without additional damage.

1

Secure & Assess

We secure the door in place and assess the full damage — track condition, roller state, cable and spring integrity, and panel alignment before starting any repair.

2

Straighten the Track

If the track is bent, we carefully straighten and realign it. Severely damaged tracks are replaced entirely to prevent the door from going off-track again.

3

Reinstall the Door

We carefully guide each panel and roller back onto the track, checking alignment at every point to ensure the door sits evenly and moves freely.

4

Test & Address Root Cause

We run full open/close cycles, check cable and spring tension, and address whatever caused the door to go off-track — so it doesn't happen again.
